content.text: I need a utility function to reliably split an API route path into its directory prefix and the final resource name.

This approach uses string splitting from the right side of the URL using '/' as the delimiter. This ensures that even complex deep paths are handled correctly by isolating the last component for 'name'.

```python
def split_api_filepath(path):
    """
    Split an API file path into directory and name.
    """
    parts = path.rsplit('/', 1)
    if len(parts) == 1:
        name = parts[0]
        dirname = '/'
    else:
        name = parts[1]
        dirname = parts[0] + '/'

    return from_api_dirname(dirname), name
```

If the path starts with a leading `/`, the function correctly identifies this as part of the directory structure. Note that it relies on an external function `from_api_dirname` to format the resulting directory string.
What happens if I pass in just '/' or '/my-resource'?
If you pass '/', both dirname and name become derived from '//'. For '/my-resource', parts will be ['', 'my-resource'], correctly assigning the dir as '/' and the name as 'my-resource'.
That makes perfect sense, thanks for clarifying how it handles those paths!
